chunk_summary: This section outlines the classification of thinness in school-age children and adolescents using BMI-for-age z-scores, highlights red flags for medical evaluation, and emphasizes follow-up protocols for undernutrition screening.
learning_objectives: Understand the use of BMI-for-age z-scores (BAZ) for assessing thinness in ages 5–19 years.
Identify red flags in adolescents that warrant medical evaluation and referral.
Learn follow-up protocols for monitoring weight trajectory and height velocity in cases of thinness or severe thinness.
key_facts: Thinness is classified as BMI-for-age z-score (BAZ) < -2 to ≥ -3 SD, and severe thinness as BAZ < -3 SD.
Weight-for-age is not a reliable indicator for acute-risk decisions in children over 10 years.
Red flags include rapid weight loss, delayed puberty, chronic gastrointestinal symptoms, and signs of disordered eating.
Follow-up for thinness should occur within 4–6 weeks; severe thinness or red flags require sooner reassessment.
Height velocity should be compared to familial range using mid-parental height to rule out endocrine or systemic disease.
reference_quote: BMI-for-age z-scores (BAZ) from the WHO 2007 growth reference are used to assess acute undernutrition in ages 5–19 years, not weight-for-height.
document_key_topics: Follow-up and monitoring protocols for thinness
Height velocity assessment in short stature
